main_summary.summary: Sam Mahone, born in 1945 in Americus, Sumter County, Georgia, recounts his entry into Civil Rights activism while in high school through the Americus Voters League and his subsequent recruitment into SNCC when Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen arrived in late 1962, dispatched from Albany by project director Charles Sherrod. Mahone describes voter registration canvassing, Freedom School organizing, and the 1963 movie-theater protest that led to his arrest, a 90-day sentence at the Sumter County prison farm, and a seven-day hunger strike in the Hole. He describes the Americus Four case, in which Don Harris, John Perdew, Ralph Allen, and CORE worker Zev Aelony were charged under a century-old sedition law carrying the death penalty before a three-judge federal panel released them; the legal team included C.B. King, Donald Hollowell, and Constance Baker. Mahone also recounts the Leesburg stockade detention of juvenile girls, the segregated-voting-line case brought by Mary Kate Bell Fishbel and Mamie Campbell in 1965, Maids Union organizing, his Tougaloo enrollment in 1966, draft into the Army in 1969, and post-1971 career at the Georgia Department of Archives and as a photographer. He describes the 2007 founding of a biennial Americus Movement commemoration at Bottoms Funeral Home.
key_themes: SNCC voter registration in Southwest Georgia
Americus Movement and the Americus Four sedition case
Incarceration and the Sumter County prison farm
Leesburg stockade and the Stolen Girls
Movement memory and oral history preservation
main_summary.historical_significance:
Mahone provides a first-person account of the Americus, Georgia SNCC project from a local high-school recruit's perspective, complementing the Albany Movement and Mississippi Freedom Summer narratives. His testimony documents the Americus Four sedition prosecution (a death-penalty case that, had it succeeded, would have allowed police to detain civil rights workers indefinitely without cause) and the Leesburg stockade detention of juvenile girls in 1963, including the kidnapping-by-tractor-trailer episode that journalists later termed the 'Stolen Girls.'

The interview also documents the 1965 segregated-voting-line lawsuit brought by Mary Kate Bell Fishbel, Mamie Campbell, Gloria Wise, and Lena Turner, and Mahone's role in convening the biennial Americus Movement reunion at Bottoms Funeral Home beginning in 2007. As a SNCC field secretary, Tougaloo student, and later a paper-conservation apprentice at the Georgia Department of Archives, Mahone occupies an unusual position bridging movement participation and archival custodianship of movement records.

chapters.title: Childhood in Segregated Americus
chapters.summary: Mahone describes growing up in Americus, Sumter County, Georgia, born in 1945 to Burgin Mahone, a basket-factory worker, and Lula Belle Walker Mahone, a domestic. He recounts the visible markers of Jim Crow segregation: outdoor toilets until his senior year of high school, parents who avoided eye contact with whites, insurance agents who walked into Black homes uninvited, and his parents' fear-based admonitions about knowing one's place.

chapters.title: Americus Voters League and Early Activism
chapters.summary: Around 1961, while in tenth grade, Mahone joined the Americus Voters League, a minister-led voter registration project that predated SNCC's arrival in town. He talked to fellow students about voter registration and worked outside jobs including picking cotton, peanuts, and peaches. His parents were quietly supportive though fearful; his older siblings had left home for the military.

chapters.title: SNCC Arrives in Americus
chapters.summary: In late 1962, Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen arrived in Americus, dispatched from Albany by project director Charles Sherrod. Mahone describes the SNCC house as a hangout where students printed leaflets on mimeograph and hand-crank machines, encountered Black authors like Richard Wright and James Baldwin for the first time, and trained in nonviolent self-defense techniques including how to curl into a fetal position when beaten.

chapters.title: Movie Theater Arrest and the Hole
chapters.summary: Mahone describes the 1963 protest at the downtown Americus movie theater, where eleven students attempted to buy tickets at the front entrance instead of using the side-alley Black entrance. After a second arrest a week later, four of the original eleven were sent to the Sumter County prison farm. Mahone and another student were placed in the Hole, a 4-by-6-foot concrete enclosure, where they conducted a seven-day hunger strike until they passed out.

chapters.title: Sheriff Chappell and Jail Conditions
chapters.summary: Mahone identifies Sheriff Fred Chappell, who jailed Dr. King in 1961, as the figure King called the meanest man in the world. He describes daily forced labor on Americus streets and at the city sewage treatment plants, contact with passersby who relayed movement news, and the relationships formed with non-movement Black inmates who treated the young protesters as little brothers.

chapters.title: Joining SNCC and the Hasty House Attack
chapters.summary: After high school, Mahone formally joined SNCC and continued voter registration across surrounding counties. On the night the Civil Rights Bill was signed in July 1964, he, John Perdew, Bob Mants, and other students attempted to test the law at the Hasty House restaurant in Americus. They were attacked by a group of white men with tire irons and baseball bats; one of their party who had gone to the outside restroom was separately beaten.

chapters.title: The Americus Four Sedition Case
chapters.summary: In 1963, during a night demonstration, Don Harris, John Perdew, Ralph Allen of SNCC, and CORE worker Zev Aelony were arrested and charged under a century-old Georgia sedition law carrying the death penalty. They were held roughly 85 days before a three-judge federal panel released them. The legal team included C.B. King of Albany, Donald Hollowell of Atlanta, and Constance Baker. Mahone notes that had the state prevailed, police nationwide could have detained movement workers indefinitely.

chapters.title: Leesburg Stockade and the Stolen Girls
chapters.summary: As the Americus jails filled with demonstrators, juvenile girls under sixteen were taken in a tractor trailer in the middle of the night to the abandoned Leesburg, Georgia prison camp. The stockade had one toilet for 35-40 girls, no bedding, and no showers. Mahone recounts a guard throwing a rattlesnake into the cell, white men coming to the jail seeking access to the girls, and the parents' days-long ignorance of where their daughters had been taken.

chapters.title: Maids Union and Segregated Voting Lines
chapters.summary: Mahone describes 1964-65 organizing work: a Maids Union formed by mothers of the incarcerated girls to demand better wages, a 20,000-book community library called the U-Center housed in the former Colored Hospital, and a quilting union. In 1965, Mary Kate Bell Fishbel qualified as the first Black woman for the Sumter County Democratic primary; she and three others (Mamie Campbell, Gloria Wise, Lena Turner) were jailed for trying to vote in the white line. A federal judge released them and declared segregated voting lines in Sumter unconstitutional.

chapters.title: Tougaloo and Warren Fortson
chapters.summary: In 1966 Mahone moved to Mississippi on a SNCC scholarship to Tougaloo College, where he protested the war in Vietnam. He also describes Warren Fortson, a white Americus lawyer and school-board head who defended Roberta Freeman (one of the first Black students to integrate Americus High) and her boyfriend on fabricated charges, only to be hounded out of town by the Klan. Fortson's brother Ben Fortson was Georgia Secretary of State.

chapters.title: Archives Work and Military Service
chapters.summary: Through Warren Fortson's referral, Mahone took an apprenticeship in paper conservation at the Georgia Department of Archives and stayed roughly 12-15 years. Drafted in 1969, he was held at Fort Benning for a year because his Americus arrest record blocked security clearance, performing menial duty work alongside Vietnam-draft refusers. Shipped to a US Army Materiel Command post outside Paris, France for the rest of his service, he took up photography on weekends and was discharged in 1971.

chapters.title: Ancestral Arts Gallery and Movement Memory
chapters.summary: Mahone opened Ancestral Arts Gallery in Atlanta as a venue for African American artists marginalized by traditional galleries, also using it for poetry sessions and public meetings. In 2007, with other Americus Movement veterans, he convened a biennial reunion at Bottoms Funeral Home, whose director had been the sole bail bondsman for the movement. The group collects photographs, newsletters (including the Voice of Americus mimeograph), and reproductions from the Atlanta Journal, Columbus Ledger-Enquirer, Macon Telegraph, and Atlanta Daily World, working toward a museum and an oral history project with the local university in Jimmy Carter's hometown.
